steps.title: Identify the sentence structure and function of 'though'
steps.content: The sentence contains an independent clause—“Power plants like Platanares supply only a small fraction of Honduras's electricity”—followed by the word “though” and then a noun phrase describing the limiting reason: “the scarcity of sites with the necessary combination of high-temperature geothermal reservoirs and fractured rock limiting the widespread development of such systems.” The noun phrase is not an independent clause (it has no finite verb; “limiting” is a participle). The word “though” here is used postpositively—at the end of the main clause—to introduce a contrasting reason that explains why supply is limited.

steps.title: Apply the postpositive 'though' punctuation rule
steps.content:
When “though” is used postpositively and followed by additional information, it must be set off with commas on both sides: IC, though, [additional info]. This punctuation treats “though” as a parenthetical transition word connecting the main clause to the explanatory noun phrase. Choice A misuses a semicolon before “though” and choice B places a semicolon after it; semicolons connect only independent clauses, and the following part is a noun phrase. Choice C omits the comma after “though,” leaving it to connect directly to the noun phrase as a subordinating conjunction, which breaks the parenthetical framing the sentence requires.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
